jsonLdWeb.name: Grain Bin Calculator
jsonLdWeb.description: Calculate grain bin storage capacity in bushels, tons, and cubic feet for multiple grain types.

mainEntity.name: How do you calculate grain bin bushels?

acceptedAnswer.text: Calculate the cylinder volume: π × radius² × height (all in feet) to get cubic feet. Then multiply by the packing factor for your grain type: corn uses 0.8 bushels per cubic foot (1 bushel = 1.25 cu ft).

mainEntity.name: How many bushels fit in a grain bin?

acceptedAnswer.text: A 24-foot diameter by 18-foot tall bin holds about 5,655 bushels of corn. The formula is: π × (diameter/2)² × height × 0.8 bushels per cubic foot for corn.

mainEntity.name: What is the test weight for different grains?

acceptedAnswer.text: Standard test weights: Corn = 56 lbs/bushel, Wheat = 60 lbs/bushel, Soybeans = 60 lbs/bushel, Oats = 32 lbs/bushel. These are used to convert bushels to tons.

mainEntity.name: How do you convert bushels to tons?

acceptedAnswer.text: Multiply bushels by the test weight per bushel, then divide by 2,000 for short tons. For corn: bushels × 56 ÷ 2,000. For wheat: bushels × 60 ÷ 2,000.

mainEntity.name: Does peak grain height affect capacity?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. When grain is loaded from center, it forms a cone (peak) that adds about 15-20% of one ring's capacity. This calculator uses the eave height; actual capacity is slightly more when grain is peaked.

mainEntity.name: What size grain bin do I need for my farm?

acceptedAnswer.text: Plan for your average yield per acre times your acres. A 1,000-acre corn farm yielding 180 bu/acre produces 180,000 bushels. You would need approximately 3-4 large bins (48 ft × 18 ft rings) to store a full crop.

howItWorks:
How the Grain Bin Calculator Works

This calculator uses the cylinder volume formula along with grain-specific packing factors to determine bin capacity.

Bushels = π × r² × h × 0.8 (for corn)
  • Volume: π × (diameter/2)² × height = cubic feet
  • Corn: 0.80 bu/cu ft, 56 lbs/bu
  • Wheat: 0.80 bu/cu ft, 60 lbs/bu
  • Soybeans: 0.80 bu/cu ft, 60 lbs/bu
  • Oats: 0.80 bu/cu ft, 32 lbs/bu
  • Tons: Bushels × test weight ÷ 2,000
faqHTML:
Frequently Asked Questions

How do you calculate grain bin bushels?

Calculate the cylinder volume in cubic feet (π × radius² × height), then multiply by the packing factor of 0.8 bushels per cubic foot. One bushel equals 1.25 cubic feet regardless of grain type.

How many bushels fit in a grain bin?

A 24-foot diameter by 18-foot tall bin holds about 5,800-6,500 bushels of grain. Capacity depends on how full the bin is and whether the grain is peaked or leveled at the eave height.

What is the test weight for different grains?

Standard test weights are: corn at 56 lbs/bushel, wheat and soybeans at 60 lbs/bushel, and oats at 32 lbs/bushel. These weights are used to convert bushel volume to weight in tons.

How do you convert bushels to tons?

Multiply the number of bushels by the grain's test weight (lbs per bushel), then divide by 2,000 to get short tons. For example, 1,000 bushels of corn = 1,000 × 56 ÷ 2,000 = 28 tons.

Does peak grain height affect capacity?

Yes, when grain is loaded from the center it forms a cone that adds extra capacity above the eave line. This can add 15-20% more storage. This calculator uses eave height for conservative estimates.

What size grain bin do I need for my farm?

Multiply your average yield per acre by total acres to get total bushels needed. A 1,000-acre corn farm at 180 bu/acre produces 180,000 bushels and would need multiple large bins for full storage.
